refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement boundary (what constitutes 'available'), (2) the evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year), (3) exclusions (planned maintenance windows, customer-initiated pauses), and (4) the role of safety-driven shutdowns. High safety criticality demands that availability not be achieved at the expense of safety; therefore, safety-driven unavailability is acceptable and must be distinguished from failure-driven unavailability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, failure events, maintenance records) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Unplanned Downtime − Safety Shutdowns) / Total Time. Compare against the ≥0.97 threshold. Conduct root-cause analysis on any downtime events to distinguish between design defects, operational errors, and safety interventions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (failures, faults, anomalies) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive and preventive maintenance strategies to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status and performance metrics to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall ensure that safety-driven shutdowns are logged separately and do not penalize the availability KPI calculation.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized availability monitoring and analytics engine that continuously tracks system state (operational, maintenance, failed, safety-shutdown), logs all transitions with timestamps, and calculates rolling availability metrics against the target threshold.

DP-B: Distribute availability monitoring across Vehicle health monitoring, Teleoperation status tracking, and Safety Services shutdown events, with periodic aggregation and reporting to a central dashboard.
DP-C: Implement redundant subsystems (dual motors, dual control channels, dual safety circuits) to minimize single-point failures and achieve high availability through hardware redundancy alone.

DP-D: Combine centralized monitoring (DP-A) with predictive maintenance algorithms that analyze telemetry trends to forecast failures and trigger preventive maintenance before unplanned downtime occurs.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a centralized availability monitoring and analytics engine (DP-A foundation) augmented with predictive maintenance algorithms that analyze Vehicle telemetry (engine load, temperature, vibration, cycle counts) and Safety Services event logs to forecast component degradation, trigger preventive maintenance alerts to the Maintenance team, and maintain a real-time availability dashboard accessible to Site Manager and Tele-operator. This DP balances reactive monitoring (detecting failures) with proactive intervention (preventing failures), directly addressing the high safety criticality by reducing unplanned downtime while maintaining safety-driven shutdown integrity.

potential_sources: Coupling between availability monitoring and Vehicle telemetry collection: DP-D depends on continuous, reliable telemetry from Vehicle subsystems. If telemetry is incomplete or delayed, predictive accuracy degrades. Mitigation: define telemetry SLAs (latency, completeness) as part of the Telemetry interface contract.

Coupling between predictive maintenance and Maintenance team responsiveness: DP-D generates alerts, but availability KPI is only met if the Maintenance team acts on alerts within a defined window. Mitigation: establish maintenance response time SLAs and escalation procedures.

Coupling between Safety Services and availability calculation: Safety-driven shutdowns must be correctly classified to avoid penalizing availability. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface must include shutdown reason codes (safety vs. failure).
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is decoupled from the specific Vehicle architecture (motor type, control method) and from Teleoperation operational decisions, making it broadly applicable. The main coupling is data-driven (telemetry, events) rather than control-driven, which is manageable via interface contracts.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Telemetry data (engine load, temperature, vibration, cycle counts) is available from Vehicle subsystems with sufficient fidelity and latency to enable predictive modeling.
ASSUMPTION: Historical failure data and maintenance records are available to train predictive models and establish degradation thresholds.
ASSUMPTION: Maintenance team has capacity and authority to respond to preventive maintenance alerts within a defined SLA (e.g., within 24 hours for high-priority alerts).

RISK: Predictive models may have false-positive rates (alerting for failures that do not occur), leading to unnecessary maintenance and cost overruns. Mitigation: validate models against historical data; implement feedback loops to refine thresholds.

RISK: If telemetry is unavailable or degraded (e.g., due to network issues), predictive capability is lost and availability may degrade. Mitigation: implement fallback to reactive monitoring and define graceful degradation behavior.

RISK: Safety-driven shutdowns may be frequent in high-risk environments, making the ≥0.97 target difficult to achieve if not properly excluded from the availability calculation. Mitigation: clarify with customer the definition of 'availability' and the treatment of safety shutdowns.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) the set of telemetry parameters required from Vehicle (e.g., engine load, temperature, vibration, cycle counts, fault codes), (2) the predictive models or algorithms to be used (e.g., time-series anomaly detection, remaining useful life estimation), (3) the alert thresholds and escalation procedures, (4) the maintenance response SLA, and (5) the availability calculation formula and the treatment of safety shutdowns. This will reduce ambiguity and enable clearer IM assignment and verification.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Safety Services is the designated implementation module for the availability monitoring and predictive maintenance engine. It owns the responsibility for: (1) collecting and aggregating telemetry from Vehicle via the Telemetry interface, (2) running predictive maintenance algorithms to forecast component degradation, (3) generating and routing SafetyAlert messages (including maintenance alerts and availability status) to the Site Manager and Maintenance team, (4) maintaining the real-time availability dashboard, (5) logging all availability-related events (uptime, downtime, maintenance actions, safety shutdowns) for post-evaluation analysis, and (6) ensuring that safety-driven shutdowns are correctly classified and excluded from the availability KPI calculation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services receives continuous telemetry streams from Vehicle (engine load, temperature, vibration, cycle counts, fault codes, operational state). Telemetry is used to train and run predictive maintenance models. Required SLA: latency ≤5 seconds, completeness ≥99% over evaluation period.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services sends SafetyAlert messages to Site Manager, Tele-operator, and Maintenance team. Alert types include: (1) predictive maintenance alerts (component degradation forecast, recommended action, urgency level), (2) availability status updates (current availability %, trend, forecast), (3) safety shutdown notifications (reason code, duration, impact on availability). Alert format must include timestamp, source, severity, and reason code.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services may receive maintenance task assignments or confirmations from the Maintenance team via Tasking interface, enabling closed-loop feedback on whether preventive maintenance alerts were acted upon and the outcome (success, partial, deferred).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services relies on Network Link for reliable communication with Vehicle (telemetry ingestion), Site Manager (dashboard access, alerts), and Maintenance team (alert delivery, task feedback). Network Link must provide sufficient bandwidth and reliability to support real-time monitoring and alerting.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary. Availability monitoring is a system-level concern that spans multiple subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would result in high diffusion (the monitoring logic would be scattered across multiple subsystems or duplicated). Not recommended as the primary IM.

Vehicle is responsible for mechanical and control subsystems (motors, sensors, actuators). While Vehicle generates telemetry and experiences failures, it is not the natural owner of system-level availability monitoring and predictive maintenance algorithms. Assigning IM to Vehicle would couple availability logic tightly to Vehicle implementation, reducing reusability and creating diffusion if multiple Vehicle types are supported. Not recommended.

Teleoperation is responsible for remote operation and control of the Vehicle. It is not responsible for availability monitoring or predictive maintenance. Assigning IM to Teleoperation would be a poor fit and would create high diffusion. Not recommended.

Safety Services is the natural owner of availability monitoring and predictive maintenance. Safety Services already owns safety-critical monitoring, alerting, and shutdown logic. Availability monitoring is closely related to safety (high safety criticality per risk tailoring factors). Safety Services can integrate telemetry collection, predictive modeling, alert generation, and availability dashboard within a cohesive module. Diffusion is minimal because the logic is centralized in one module. Strongly recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the optimal IM boundary for DP-D because: (1) it is the designated owner of safety-critical monitoring and alerting, and availability is a safety-critical KPI; (2) it has natural access to telemetry via the Telemetry interface and can generate alerts via SafetyAlert; (3) it can integrate predictive maintenance algorithms, availability calculation, and dashboard logic without diffusion; (4) it can correctly classify and exclude safety-driven shutdowns from the availability KPI, maintaining the integrity of the metric; (5) it can coordinate with the Maintenance team via Tasking interface to close the loop on preventive maintenance actions.
containment_risks: RISK: Safety Services may become overloaded if it takes on too many responsibilities (safety monitoring, availability monitoring, predictive maintenance, alerting, dashboard). Mitigation: clearly define the scope of Safety Services and ensure it has sufficient computational resources and team capacity.

RISK: If Safety Services is tightly coupled to a specific Vehicle type or Teleoperation mode, the availability monitoring logic may not be reusable across different NGHE configurations. Mitigation: design Safety Services to be agnostic to Vehicle and Teleoperation implementation details; use the Telemetry interface as the abstraction boundary.

RISK: Predictive maintenance algorithms may require domain expertise (e.g., mechanical engineering, data science) that may not be available within the Safety Services team. Mitigation: establish partnerships with domain experts or use third-party libraries for predictive modeling.

RISK: If the Maintenance team does not have the capacity or authority to respond to preventive maintenance alerts, the availability KPI may not be achieved. Mitigation: establish clear SLAs and escalation procedures; involve the Maintenance/Quality stakeholder in the design and validation of the alert thresholds.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm with the Safety Services team and the Maintenance/Quality stakeholder that Safety Services is the appropriate owner of the availability monitoring and predictive maintenance engine. Define clear interfaces (Telemetry input, SafetyAlert output, Tasking feedback) and SLAs (telemetry latency, alert delivery time, maintenance response time). Establish a governance model for updating predictive models and alert thresholds based on operational feedback. Consider whether a separate 'Availability Analytics' module should be created as a sub-component of Safety Services to isolate the predictive modeling logic from the core safety monitoring logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (centralized monitoring + predictive maintenance) directly addresses the refined FR (achieve ≥0.97 availability). The DP is specific enough to guide implementation (telemetry collection, predictive algorithms, alerting, dashboard) while remaining flexible enough to accommodate different Vehicle types and operational scenarios. The DP explicitly accounts for the high safety criticality by ensuring that safety-driven shutdowns are correctly classified and do not penalize the availability KPI. The coupling check identified manageable data-driven couplings (telemetry, events) rather than control-driven couplings. Assumptions and risks are documented and mitigatable.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If Safety Services does not have sufficient computational resources or team capacity, the availability monitoring logic may be offloaded to Vehicle or Teleoperation, creating diffusion. Mitigation: allocate adequate resources to Safety Services and establish clear ownership boundaries.

Diffusion risk: If predictive maintenance algorithms are implemented separately from the core Safety Services logic (e.g., in a third-party analytics platform), the availability monitoring may become fragmented. Mitigation: integrate predictive modeling tightly within Safety Services or establish clear data contracts between Safety Services and external analytics platforms.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services has a containment score of 0.9 and diffusion score of 0.1, indicating strong alignment. The module naturally owns safety-critical monitoring and alerting, and availability monitoring is a natural extension of this responsibility. The Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, and Network Link interfaces provide clear boundaries for data flow. The main containment risk is scope creep (Safety Services taking on too many responsibilities), which can be mitigated by clear scope definition and resource allocation.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of the refined FR (achieve ≥0.97 availability) is feasible if Safety Services correctly implements the availability monitoring and calculation logic. Verification requires: (1) collection of operational telemetry and event logs over the agreed evaluation period, (2) calculation of availability using the agreed formula (excluding planned maintenance and safety shutdowns), (3) comparison against the ≥0.97 threshold, (4) root-cause analysis of any downtime events. Safety Services must provide audit trails and dashboards to support verification. Verification is dependent on the Maintenance team's responsiveness to preventive maintenance alerts; if the Maintenance team does not act on alerts, the availability KPI may not be achieved despite correct DP and IM implementation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of the refined FR to Safety Services is clear. Safety Services owns the responsibility for: (1) collecting telemetry from Vehicle, (2) running predictive maintenance algorithms, (3) generating alerts to the Maintenance team, (4) maintaining the availability dashboard, (5) calculating and reporting the availability KPI. The sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) are allocated as follows: FR-NGHE-004.1 (detect and log downtime) → Safety Services; FR-NGHE-004.2 (implement predictive maintenance) → Safety Services; FR-NGHE-004.3 (provide availability status) → Safety Services; FR-NGHE-004.4 (ensure safety shutdowns are logged separately) → Safety Services. All sub-FRs are contained within Safety Services, with no diffusion.

recommended_revisions: Refine the availability calculation formula to explicitly define: (1) the measurement period (e.g., rolling 30-day window, calendar month, fiscal quarter), (2) the treatment of planned maintenance (excluded or included), (3) the treatment of safety-driven shutdowns (excluded or included), (4) the treatment of partial availability (e.g., if one of two motors fails, is the system 50% available or 0% available?). This will reduce ambiguity and enable consistent verification across different evaluation periods.

Establish clear SLAs for the Maintenance team's response to preventive maintenance alerts (e.g., high-priority alerts within 24 hours, medium-priority within 72 hours). Include these SLAs in the contract with the Maintenance/Quality stakeholder.

Define the set of telemetry parameters required from Vehicle (e.g., engine load, temperature, vibration, cycle counts, fault codes) and the latency/completeness SLAs for each parameter. This will enable Safety Services to design the predictive models with confidence.

Establish a governance model for updating predictive models and alert thresholds based on operational feedback. This will ensure that the availability monitoring logic evolves with operational experience and maintains accuracy over time.

decision.justification: The refined FR, selected DP, and assigned IM are well-aligned and pass all gate reviews. However, the refined FR includes four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) that require further decomposition to guide detailed design and implementation. Each sub-FR addresses a specific aspect of the availability monitoring and predictive maintenance system (downtime detection, predictive maintenance, status reporting, safety shutdown classification). Decomposing these sub-FRs will clarify the detailed requirements for Safety Services and enable more precise IM assignment and verification planning.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (failures, faults, anomalies) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive and preventive maintenance strategies to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status and performance metrics to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall ensure that safety-driven shutdowns are logged separately and do not penalize the availability KPI calculation.

assumptions: Telemetry data (engine load, temperature, vibration, cycle counts, fault codes) is available from Vehicle subsystems with sufficient fidelity and latency (≤5 seconds) to enable predictive modeling.
Historical failure data and maintenance records are available to train predictive models and establish degradation thresholds.
Maintenance team has capacity and authority to respond to preventive maintenance alerts within a defined SLA (e.g., within 24 hours for high-priority alerts).
Safety-driven shutdowns can be reliably distinguished from failure-driven shutdowns via SafetyAlert reason codes.
The customer-defined operational availability target is ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period (to be confirmed with customer).
Safety Services has sufficient computational resources and team capacity to implement and maintain the availability monitoring and predictive maintenance engine.
open_risks: Predictive models may have false-positive rates (alerting for failures that do not occur), leading to unnecessary maintenance and cost overruns.
If telemetry is unavailable or degraded (e.g., due to network issues), predictive capability is lost and availability may degrade.
Safety-driven shutdowns may be frequent in high-risk environments, making the ≥0.97 target difficult to achieve if not properly excluded from the availability calculation.
If the Maintenance team does not have the capacity or authority to respond to preventive maintenance alerts, the availability KPI may not be achieved despite correct DP and IM implementation.
Safety Services may become overloaded if it takes on too many responsibilities, leading to performance degradation or quality issues.
Predictive maintenance algorithms may require domain expertise (e.g., mechanical engineering, data science) that may not be available within the Safety Services team.
evidence_needed: Confirmation from the customer on the definition of 'availability' and the treatment of planned maintenance and safety-driven shutdowns.
Confirmation from the Safety Services team that it is the appropriate owner of the availability monitoring and predictive maintenance engine.
Confirmation from the Maintenance/Quality stakeholder on the maintenance response SLAs and the capacity to respond to preventive maintenance alerts.
Specification of the telemetry parameters required from Vehicle and the latency/completeness SLAs for each parameter.
Historical failure data and maintenance records to validate predictive models and establish degradation thresholds.
Detailed design of the predictive maintenance algorithms (e.g., time-series anomaly detection, remaining useful life estimation) and the alert thresholds.
Detailed design of the availability dashboard and the reporting interface to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
Governance model for updating predictive models and alert thresholds based on operational feedback.
